title: Spain
subtitle: 1808–1900 · Peninsular War, Constitutions and the Long Crisis

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: A century of instability — 43 governments in 92 years

entries.label: Joseph I and Ferdinand VII — war and absolutism
entries.hint: Napoleon replaces the Bourbons with his brother Joseph I in 1808 — triggering the Peninsular War; Ferdinand VII returns in 1814 and immediately abolishes the 1812 Constitution; his decade-long absolutism, interrupted by the Trienio Liberal (1820–1823), ends with the First Carlist War breaking out at his death.
entries.note: Joseph I's appointment: Gaceta de Madrid 20 Jun 1808. Ferdinand VII's abolition of the 1812 Constitution: Royal Decree 4 May 1814, Gaceta de Madrid. The Trienio Liberal (1820–1823): Riego's pronunciamiento 1 Jan 1820 — documented in AGS. Carr (1982), Spain 1808–1975: the essential survey of the century. The 43 governments: counted in Solé Tura & Aja (1977), Constituciones y períodos constituyentes en España.

entries.label: Isabel II — the liberal monarchy
entries.hint: Isabel II reigns through two Carlist Wars, multiple constitutions, and 40 years of liberal-conservative oscillation — she is overthrown by the Glorious Revolution of September 1868 and goes into exile; Spain then tries a constitutional monarchy with a foreign king, a republic, and a military coup before returning to the Bourbons.
entries.note: Isabel II's regency under her mother María Cristina (1833–1843): documented in Gaceta de Madrid. The Glorious Revolution 18 Sep 1868: press records and AGS. Burdiel (2010), Isabel II: the standard modern biography. The constitutional sequence: 1837, 1845, 1869 — all published in Gaceta de Madrid.

entries.label: Alfonso XII and the Bourbon Restoration
entries.hint: Alfonso XII's restoration (1874) inaugurates the Cánovas turno pacífico — an arranged alternation of Liberal and Conservative parties that creates apparent political stability through managed elections (caciquismo); the system holds until 1898 when colonial disaster breaks it.
entries.note: Alfonso XII's Pronunciamiento of Sagunto 29 Dec 1874: Gaceta de Madrid. The Constitution of 1876: Gaceta de Madrid 30 Jun 1876. The turno pacífico system: Cánovas's design documented in his writings. Varela Ortega (1977), Los amigos políticos: the foundational study of caciquismo. Alfonso XII dies 1885; regency of María Cristina until 1902.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Peninsular War, Carlist Wars and colonial collapse

entries.label: Dos de Mayo — the uprising that begins the Peninsular War
entries.hint: The Madrid population rises against French occupation on 2 May 1808 — Murat's cavalry suppresses the uprising; executions follow on 3 May; Goya documents both days in two of the most powerful paintings in Western art; the national war begins the next day across Spain.
entries.note: Goya, The Second of May 1808 and The Third of May 1808 (painted c.1814): Prado (P000748, P000749). The uprising: documented in contemporary French military reports (Archives de la Guerre, Paris) and Spanish sources. Esdaile (2003), The Peninsular War: the standard modern study. The juntas that formed after 2 May: documented in AGS.

entries.label: Battle of Bailén — Napoleon's first European defeat
entries.hint: Spanish forces under General Castaños defeat and capture an entire French army corps at Bailén — Napoleon's first defeat in Europe; it shatters the myth of French invincibility and inspires resistance across Europe.
entries.note: Spanish military records: AGS Guerra. French records: Archives de la Guerre, Paris — Dupont's surrender documents. Contemporary impact across Europe: documented in press records (Times of London, 23 Aug 1808). Esdaile (2003), Peninsular War, ch.3. The 18,000 French prisoners: their fate documented in AGS.

entries.label: First Carlist War — dynastic civil war
entries.hint: On Ferdinand VII's death, his brother Carlos claims the throne against his daughter Isabel — triggering a seven-year civil war fought primarily in the Basque Country and Navarre; the Carlists represent traditionalism (Church, fueros, monarchy); the Liberals represent constitutional modernisation.
entries.note: The war's military records: AGS Guerra Moderna. The Convention of Vergara (31 Aug 1839): the peace agreement — text in Gaceta de Madrid 1 Sep 1839. Holt (1967), The Carlist Wars in Spain: the standard English study. Three Carlist Wars in total (1833–1840, 1846–1849, 1872–1876) make 19th-century Spain's civil conflict the longest in Western European history.

entries.label: Disaster of '98 — Cuba, Puerto Rico and the Philippines lost
entries.hint: Spain is defeated by the United States in 10 weeks — Cuba, Puerto Rico and the Philippines are lost; Spain's last remnants of empire are gone; the 'Generation of '98' intellectual movement responds with a crisis of national identity that shapes the entire 20th century.
entries.note: Treaty of Paris 10 Dec 1898: US Senate ratification records; Spanish text in Gaceta de Madrid. The naval battles: US Navy records (NARA) and Spanish AGS Marina. Tone (2006), War and Genocide in Cuba 1895–1898. The Spanish navy's losses at Santiago de Cuba 3 Jul 1898: documented in AGS Marina — complete destruction of the Caribbean fleet. This entry bridges directly to the spain-1900-1975 folio.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Spain's constitutional laboratory — six constitutions in one century

entries.label: Constitution of 1812 — La Pepa
entries.hint: The Cádiz Constitution is proclaimed on Saint Joseph's Day (hence 'La Pepa') — the first Spanish liberal constitution; it establishes national sovereignty, abolishes the Inquisition, ends feudal privileges, and provides for freedom of the press; it becomes the model for liberal constitutions across southern Europe and Latin America.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Original text in Archivo de las Cortes (Madrid) and Archivo Municipal de Cádiz. Published in Gaceta de la Regencia de España e Indias, 19 Mar 1812. Fernández Sarasola (2002), La Constitución de Cádiz: the standard modern study. The constitution's international influence: explicitly cited in the Portuguese (1822), Italian (1820) and Norwegian (1814) constitutions.

entries.label: Six constitutions — Spain's constitutional instability
entries.hint: Spain enacts six different constitutions in one century — each representing a different political settlement between liberals and conservatives; no constitution lasts more than 30 years; the instability is itself the primary historical fact of 19th-century Spanish politics.
entries.note: All six texts: Gaceta de Madrid (each promulgation date). Solé Tura & Aja (1977), Constituciones y períodos constituyentes en España: the standard comparative study. The 1869 Constitution (the most liberal, granting universal male suffrage, religious freedom): Gaceta de Madrid 6 Jun 1869. The 1876 Constitution (the Restoration settlement, lasting to 1931): Gaceta de Madrid 30 Jun 1876.

entries.label: Abolition of the Spanish Inquisition — 1834
entries.hint: The Inquisition is abolished for the third and final time on 15 July 1834 — it had been abolished by Joseph Bonaparte (1808), restored by Ferdinand VII (1814), and finally suppressed definitively; its 356 years of operation are formally ended by a single royal decree.
entries.note: Royal Decree 15 Jul 1834: Gaceta de Madrid 16 Jul 1834. The abolitions and restorations documented in: 1808 (Joseph I): Gaceta de Madrid; 1813 (Cádiz Cortes): ibid.; 1814 (Ferdinand VII): ibid.; 1820 (Trienio): ibid.; 1823 (restored): ibid.; 1834 (final): ibid. The entire cycle is documented in consecutive Gaceta issues — a remarkable primary source for constitutional instability.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Church, state and the religious question

entries.label: Ecclesiastical confiscations — Mendizábal's desamortización
entries.hint: Prime Minister Mendizábal seizes and sells Church and monastic properties — approximately 100,000 properties auctioned; the measure finances the First Carlist War but destroys the monastic system and creates a new landowning class; the single most significant redistribution of wealth in 19th-century Spain.
entries.note: Mendizábal's decree: Gaceta de Madrid 19 Feb 1836. The confiscation records: AHN Clero (the primary archive — the most detailed surviving record of pre-industrial Spanish Church property). Tomás y Valiente (1971), El marco político de la desamortización en España: the foundational study. The 100,000 properties figure: derived from AHN Clero auction records.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Goya's Disasters, Romanticism and the realist novel

entries.label: Goya: Disasters of War — eyewitness atrocity
entries.hint: Goya's 82 etching series documents the atrocities of the Peninsular War — torture, massacre, famine, execution; printed only posthumously in 1863; they are the most powerful visual testimony of 19th-century warfare and the first anti-war art.
entries.note: Original copper plates: Real Academia de Bellas Artes de San Fernando, Madrid. First printed edition 1863 (posthumous): Biblioteca Nacional de España. Goya made them during or just after the war — their sequence and dating studied by Licht (1979). Hughes (2003), Goya, ch.12–14. The plates were never published in Goya's lifetime — possibly too dangerous under Ferdinand VII's absolutism.

entries.label: Benito Pérez Galdós — Episodios Nacionales
entries.hint: Galdós writes 46 historical novels covering Spain from Trafalgar (1805) to the Restoration — the most ambitious literary history of a century in any European language; his novels are simultaneously primary sources for social history, drawing on direct research in archives and personal memory.
entries.note: First series (Trafalgar, 1873): BNE first edition. Galdós's research methods: documented in his notebooks (Biblioteca Nacional de España, Archivo Galdós). Berkowitz (1948), Pérez Galdós: Spanish Liberal Crusader: the standard biography. The Episodios Nacionales as historical source: Bly (1983) analyses their archival basis. Note: Galdós (1843–1920) straddles both the 1808–1900 and 1900–1975 folios.

entries.label: La Verbena de la Paloma — zarzuela and popular theatre
entries.hint: Tomás Bretón's La Verbena de la Paloma (1894) is the masterpiece of the zarzuela — Spanish musical theatre combining spoken dialogue and song; its setting in working-class Madrid on the Feast of La Paloma (15 August) makes it the most detailed musical document of late 19th-century popular urban life.
entries.note: First performance Teatro Apolo, Madrid, 17 Feb 1894: documented in press records and theatre archives. Bretón's manuscript score: Sociedad General de Autores y Editores (SGAE), Madrid. Contemporary reviews: La Época, El Imparcial (Madrid press archive). Cotarelo y Mori (1934), Historia de la zarzuela: the foundational study. The libretto (Ricardo de la Vega): BNE.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Liberalism, Krausism and the roots of 20th-century Spanish thought

entries.label: Institución Libre de Enseñanza — secular education
entries.hint: Francisco Giner de los Ríos founds the Free Teaching Institution after being expelled from the University of Madrid for refusing to conform his teaching to Church and Crown orthodoxy — its graduates include García Lorca, Machado and Ortega y Gasset; it is the intellectual nursery of the Second Republic.
entries.note: Foundation act 1876: BILE (Boletín de la Institución Libre de Enseñanza) — the institution's own journal, published 1877–1936, BNE. Giner de los Ríos's expulsion from the university: documented in university records. Jiménez-Landi (1973–1996), La Institución Libre de Enseñanza (4 vols): the standard study. The ILE's alumni: documented in its membership records (BILE).

entries.label: Generation of '98 — the intellectual response to disaster
entries.hint: The '98 disaster produces a generation of writers who interrogate Spanish identity and national failure — Unamuno, Azorín, Baroja, Machado, Valle-Inclán; their essays, novels and poetry are primary evidence for elite self-questioning at the century's end.
entries.note: The term 'Generation of '98': coined by Azorín in a 1913 article — documented in press archive. Unamuno, En torno al casticismo (1895): BNE. Azorín, El alma castellana (1900): BNE. Fox (1997), Reimagining Spain: the standard English study. The generation's diverse politics (ranging from Unamuno's Catholic nationalism to Baroja's anarchism) is documented in their own correspondence.

categories.label: Subsistence
categories.subtitle: Economic backwardness, industrialisation and the agrarian question

entries.label: Catalan industrialisation — the cotton revolution
entries.hint: Catalonia industrialises around cotton textiles from the 1830s — by 1900 it has 80% of Spain's industrial capacity; the contrast between industrial Catalonia and agrarian Andalusia shapes Spanish politics for the next century.
entries.note: Factory census records: Archivo Municipal de Barcelona. The 1856 Junta de Comercio census: BNE. Nadal (1975), El fracaso de la Revolución Industrial en España: the foundational economic history of Spanish industrialisation's limits. The concentration in Catalonia: documented in National Census records (INE historical series). The contrast with the south: described in contemporary travel literature and social investigation.

entries.label: Agrarian crisis and caciquismo — the Andalusian question
entries.hint: The desamortización created a class of large landowners (latifundistas) in the south rather than small farmers — by 1900 Andalusia has the most unequal land distribution in Europe; seasonal day labourers (jornaleros) live in poverty; anarchism takes root.
entries.note: Land distribution data: Ministerio de Hacienda amillaramiento records (1860s–1880s): AHN. The jornalero condition: Costa (1902), Oligarquía y caciquismo — a primary source document based on a survey of local conditions. Bernal (1988), La lucha por la tierra en la crisis del Antiguo Régimen: the standard study. The anarchist movement's roots in Andalusian agrarian conditions: Temma Kaplan (1977), Anarchists of Andalusia.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Colonial independence, international isolation and the Atlantic world

entries.label: Spanish American independence — the empire dissolves
entries.hint: Spain loses virtually all its American empire between 1810 and 1826 — 18 new republics emerge; 300 years of colonial rule ends; Spain is left with Cuba, Puerto Rico and the Philippines; 300,000 Spanish soldiers die in the independence wars.
entries.note: Bolívar's Carta de Jamaica (6 Sep 1815): the most important programmatic document of Latin American independence — original at Archivo Nacional de Colombia. The Spanish military records: AGS Guerra. Lynch (1973), The Spanish American Revolutions: the standard study. The economic consequence for Spain: Tortella (2000), The Development of Modern Spain, ch.6.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Spain's 19th-century international isolation and colonial twilight

entries.label: Spain as Europe's 'sick man of the century'
entries.hint: Throughout the 19th century Spain is marginalised from the main currents of European development — absent from the Concert of Europe, late to industrialise, riven by civil wars; its European neighbours cease to treat it as a major power; the contrast with France (Napoleonic, industrial) and Britain (imperial, industrial) is stark.
entries.note: Spain's absence from the Concert of Europe: Kissinger (1957), A World Restored — Spain not mentioned as a Great Power. The contrast in industrial output: Mitchell (1975), European Historical Statistics — Spain's industrialisation lag documented. The 'sick man' characterisation: contemporaries including Marx (articles on Spain 1854: New-York Tribune) used this framing. Álvarez Junco (2001), Mater Dolorosa: analyses Spain's 19th-century national identity crisis.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Concepción Arenal — criminologist and feminist
entries.hint: Concepción Arenal attends university disguised as a man, becomes Spain's first female criminologist, and writes the foundational texts of Spanish prison reform and social feminism — her La mujer del porvenir (1869) is the most important 19th-century Spanish feminist text.
entries.note: La mujer del porvenir (1869): BNE first edition. Her disguise to attend university: documented in her biographies and her own later accounts. Her reports on prison conditions: Ministerio de Justicia records (AHN). Lacalzada de Mateo (1994), Mentalidad y proyección civil de Concepción Arenal: the standard biography. Her role as Visitadora de Cárceles (prison inspector): official appointment in AHN.

entries.label: Penal Code 1870 — homosexuality decriminalised de facto
entries.hint: The 1870 Liberal Penal Code removes explicit criminalisation of same-sex acts — a significant CATEGORICAL SHIFT; however social persecution continues and the 1928 Penal Code under Primo de Rivera will re-criminalise; the 1870 code documents the gap between legal toleration and social reality.
entries.note: Código Penal 1870: Gaceta de Madrid 31 Aug 1870. The removal of explicit sodomy provisions: analysed in Ugarte Pérez (2008), Una discriminación universal. The 1822 Code (first to largely secularise criminal law): Gaceta de Madrid. The 1928 re-criminalisation: Primo de Rivera's Código Penal, BOE 13 Sep 1928. The 1870–1928 period of relative legal tolerance: documented in comparison of the two codes.

events.l: Dos de Mayo — Peninsular War begins
Spain absent from Concert of Europe
